title: Why Striplane Exists
text: Striplane is a design-rule-checking and verification toolchain purpose-built for high-speed printed-circuit-board signal integrity, sitting between board layout and fabrication release. Rather than trusting a static width-and-spacing lookup table, Striplane re-derives each routed segment's characteristic impedance from the actual stackup, copper weight, and dielectric constants a design specifies, then flags any net that drifts outside its assigned tolerance band. The tool grew out of a simple observation: most impedance violations discovered during bring-up trace back to a stackup change made late in layout, after the original impedance calculations had already gone stale. Striplane keeps those calculations live throughout routing instead of treating them as a one-time pre-layout exercise. Beyond raw impedance, the toolchain now covers crosstalk between adjacent nets, via stub resonance, return-path continuity, simulated eye-diagram margin, and radiated-emissions estimation, all evaluated against the same routed geometry a fabricator will actually build.

title: Current Release Footprint
text: Striplane v9.4 shipped on April 14, 2026, the sixth minor release since the v9.0 cloud-verification-farm rollout in September 2025. The current release checks controlled-impedance nets against a default differential target of 100 ohms ±7%, tightened from the ±10% single-ended tolerance shipped in the original v1.0 release of March 2018. A full impedance-and-crosstalk DRC pass over a 24-layer, 18,000-net backplane design completes in 11 minutes on the reference eight-core verification node, down from 47 minutes measured on the equivalent v7.0 build. Striplane's installed base crossed 3,100 licensed design seats across 210 customer organizations as of Q1 2026, according to the vendor's most recent license-renewal report.

title: Controlled-Impedance Checking, Net by Net
text: Controlled-impedance checking inside Striplane runs as a live constraint attached to each net class rather than a one-off report generated after routing finishes. When a designer draws a differential pair or single-ended trace assigned to an impedance-controlled net class, Striplane continuously re-solves the local cross-section, using the routed trace width, copper thickness, dielectric height, and the specific layer's resin content, against the same field-solver core used for full-board verification. A trace that starts within tolerance can still drift out of spec if a later stackup revision thins a prepreg layer or if routing necks the trace width around a dense connector field, and Striplane catches both cases because it revalidates geometry rather than caching an earlier result. Differential pairs additionally carry a coupling term, since tightly coupled traces present a lower odd-mode impedance than the same traces routed further apart, a distinction a simple single-ended width table cannot capture.

title: Differential Impedance Targets by Board Class
text:
Striplane ships default differential-impedance targets and tolerances tuned to four board classes, summarized below.

| Board Class | Typical Layers | Diff. Z0 Target | Tolerance | Max Qualified Data Rate |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Class I (general digital) | 4-6 | 90 ohm | ±10% | 10 Gbps |
| Class II (high-speed) | 8-12 | 100 ohm | ±8% | 28 Gbps |
| Class III (backplane) | 16-24 | 95 ohm | ±7% | 56 Gbps PAM4 |
| Class IV (co-packaged optics) | 24-32 | 85 ohm | ±5% | 112 Gbps PAM4 |

Tolerance bands tighten and target impedances drop as board class moves toward higher per-lane data rates, reflecting the shrinking margin available once channel insertion loss and via stub effects start to dominate the link budget.

title: Why Striplane Uses a Closed-Form Impedance Model
text: Striplane's default impedance solver for microstrip geometry uses a closed-form approximation derived from Wheeler's equation: Z0 ≈ (87 / sqrt(er + 1.41)) * ln(5.98h / (0.8w + t)), where h is dielectric height, w is trace width, t is copper thickness, and er is the layer's dielectric constant. This closed form runs in well under a millisecond per net, letting Striplane re-check impedance continuously during interactive routing rather than only in an overnight batch job. A full 2D field solver produces a more accurate result, typically within 2% of measured impedance versus roughly 5% for the closed form, but a routed 24-layer board carries tens of thousands of segments, and invoking a field solver on every keystroke would make live checking impractical. Striplane instead reserves the field solver for final sign-off, using the closed form as a fast, good-enough gate during layout.

title: Crosstalk Engine Thresholds
text: Striplane's crosstalk engine flags any coupled-noise ratio exceeding 5% of the aggressor's peak-to-peak swing for nets routed closer than 3x trace width over a parallel-run length beyond 500 mils. The engine models near-end crosstalk (NEXT) and far-end crosstalk (FEXT) independently, evaluating coupling over a 40 GHz frequency sweep in 250 discrete steps. On the 24-layer backplane reference design, the engine reported 212 net pairs exceeding the 5% NEXT threshold before spacing remediation, reduced to 9 pairs after the routing team applied Striplane's auto-suggested 2-mil spacing increase. Crosstalk analysis alone accounts for roughly 35% of total DRC engine runtime on a typical high-speed backplane verification job.

title: Bringing a New Board Through Signal-Integrity Sign-Off
text:
Teams bringing a new board through Striplane's sign-off flow generally follow the same sequence before releasing Gerbers to fabrication.

1. Import the finalized stackup, including per-layer dielectric constant, loss tangent, and copper weight, from the fabricator's confirmed build file, not a nominal datasheet value.
2. Run the impedance DRC pass across every controlled-impedance net class and resolve every flagged segment before proceeding.
3. Run the crosstalk analysis pass on parallel-routed high-speed nets and apply any auto-suggested spacing or guard-trace changes.
4. Run the via stub checker against the confirmed back-drill depth and flag any stub exceeding the resonance-margin threshold.
5. Simulate eye-diagram margin for each SerDes channel against its mask and confirm no channel falls below the required height and width.
6. Archive the sign-off report with the fabrication package so bring-up engineers can compare bench measurements against prediction.

Skipping the via stub step is the shortcut Striplane's own support data most often ties to later bring-up escalations.

title: Via Stub Resonance in the Literature
text: Via stub resonance research outside the English-language literature has shaped several defaults in Striplane's back-drill checker. Nowak and Zieliński's 2018 paper, Rezonans kikutów przelotek w wielowarstwowych płytkach drukowanych o wysokiej gęstości upakowania, used time-domain reflectometry on densely packed backplane vias to characterize how stub length interacts with pad capacitance to shift the effective resonant frequency below the simple quarter-wave prediction. Their measurements, taken across a range of via pitches common in 16-to-24-layer backplanes, showed the correction growing more significant as pitch tightened, an effect Striplane's stub checker now applies as a small pitch-dependent derating factor rather than relying on the uncorrected quarter-wave formula alone. The Striplane engineering team credits this paper directly in the via stub checker's internal documentation, alongside several English-language backplane design guides.

title: Via Stubs and Return-Path Continuity
text: A via stub is the unused length of plated barrel left below the layer where a signal actually transitions, and at high enough frequencies that stub behaves like a small stand-alone antenna, resonating and pulling energy out of the passing signal near its resonant frequency. Striplane's via stub checker estimates each stub's length directly from the routed layer transition and the board's total thickness, then compares the predicted resonance against the channel's operating frequency with a configurable margin. Return-path continuity is checked separately but often diagnosed together with stub problems, since a signal via without a nearby, uninterrupted reference-plane via forces return current to detour around a plane split, adding inductance and radiating energy along the way. Striplane flags any signal via lacking a return-path via within a configurable radius, treating an isolated signal via without stitching as equivalent in severity to an unaddressed stub violation.

title: Eye-Diagram Correlation Data
text: Eye-diagram verification in Striplane v5.0 and later drives a virtual bit-error-rate target of 1e-15 at the receiver, sampled across a minimum eye height of 30 mV and eye width of 0.30 UI for 56 Gbps PAM4 links, per the tool's default mask library. Simulated eye closure is computed from a statistical channel model using 2^23 bits of pseudo-random pattern, correlated against physical eye-diagram captures from a 100 GHz-bandwidth sampling oscilloscope during hardware bring-up. Across 64 boards validated during the v8.0 112 Gbps qualification campaign in early 2025, simulated eye-height predictions matched bench measurements within a median error of 4.2%, with a worst-case observed deviation of 11%. Striplane logs each correlation run to a calibration database now holding over 900 board-level measurement records.

title: Eye Mask Requirements Across SerDes Generations
text:
Striplane's default eye-mask library sets minimum eye height and width requirements by SerDes generation, summarized below.

| Data Rate | Modulation | Min Eye Height | Min Eye Width | Max Insertion Loss Budget |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 10 Gbps | NRZ | 90 mV | 0.55 UI | 18 dB |
| 28 Gbps | NRZ | 55 mV | 0.45 UI | 22 dB |
| 56 Gbps | PAM4 | 30 mV | 0.30 UI | 26 dB |
| 112 Gbps | PAM4 | 18 mV | 0.22 UI | 30 dB |

Required eye height and width shrink at each successive generation even as the allowed insertion-loss budget grows, which is why Striplane weights via stub and crosstalk violations more heavily on 56 and 112 Gbps channels than on legacy 10 Gbps links.

title: The Quarter-Wave Stub Rule
text: Striplane's via stub checker is built around the classic quarter-wave resonance approximation: f_stub ≈ c / (4 * L_stub * sqrt(eeff)), where L_stub is the unused barrel length below the last active layer, eeff is the effective dielectric constant of the surrounding stackup, and c is the speed of light. The checker requires f_stub to exceed twice the channel's Nyquist frequency, f_stub >= 2 * f_nyquist, rather than the 1.5x margin common in older backplane design guides. Striplane's team adopted the tighter 2x margin after correlating predicted versus measured stub resonance across dozens of qualification boards and finding the simple quarter-wave formula under-predicted the effective resonant dip's frequency spread by up to 15%, enough to erode a 1.5x margin's safety cushion on the highest-speed channels. The 2x threshold restores that cushion without requiring every board to be fully back-drilled.

title: Differential Pair Research Beyond English-Language Venues
text: Differential pair design research published outside major English-language venues has also informed Striplane's coupling model. Nguyễn and Trần's 2020 study, Kiểm soát trở kháng đặc tính trong thiết kế cặp vi sai tốc độ cao, examined how routing-induced length mismatch between the two conductors of a differential pair degrades common-mode rejection and increases radiated emissions at connector transitions. Their measurements across a range of pair-to-pair spacings informed the default intra-pair skew limit Striplane now applies during differential-pair DRC, tightening the limit for pairs routed near connector breakouts where matched-length serpentines are hardest to fit. Striplane's EMI estimator cites the same study when explaining why skew, not just impedance mismatch, contributes measurably to common-mode radiated emissions on tightly spaced backplane connectors.

title: How Striplane's Verification Approach Evolved
text: Striplane began in 2016 as an internal width-and-spacing lookup tool built by a hardware team tired of chasing impedance violations discovered only after boards came back from fabrication. That first internal tool did nothing more than map a target impedance to a recommended trace width and spacing for a small, fixed set of stackups the team used repeatedly, and it shipped as Striplane v1.0 in March 2018 once the team decided the approach was general enough to sell externally. The lookup-table approach worked for the handful of stackups it had been tuned against, but broke down as soon as a customer specified an unfamiliar dielectric or copper weight, since the table had no entry to interpolate from. Replacing the lookup table with a closed-form field-solver approximation was the first major redesign, shipped in v1.0 itself as the underlying calculation engine but only exposed as a live, continuously re-evaluated check starting with v2.0 in 2019, which also added differential pair coupling and skew analysis. Customers adopting Striplane for backplane work quickly asked for crosstalk checking, since an impedance-clean board could still fail bring-up from coupled noise between adjacent nets, and the v3.0 crosstalk engine, released in 2020, modeled near-end and far-end coupling directly from routed geometry rather than a static spacing rule. Via stub checking followed in v4.0 the next year, prompted by a cluster of bring-up failures traced to unbackdrilled stubs on boards that had passed every impedance and crosstalk check cleanly, making clear that impedance and crosstalk alone did not capture every high-speed failure mode. Eye-diagram simulation, added in v5.0 in 2022, was the first feature to predict link-level pass/fail directly rather than flagging individual violations, and it required a statistical channel model fast enough for routine DRC use rather than only slow, offline simulation. EMI estimation arrived in v6.0 the following year, motivated by customers who kept discovering common-mode emissions failures during certification testing that none of Striplane's existing checks had flagged. The machine-learning-assisted stackup optimizer in v7.0, released 2024, was meant to recommend near-optimal stackups automatically rather than requiring manual iteration, though as a later postmortem showed, it also introduced failure modes the team had not anticipated. Cloud-based batch verification, added in v9.0 in September 2025, was less a signal-integrity feature than an operational one, letting large teams run full-board DRC sweeps automatically rather than waiting for an engineer to launch them manually. Looking back, the team describes the shift from static lookup tables to live, geometry-driven checking as the single change that made every later feature possible, since crosstalk, via stub, eye-diagram, and EMI analysis all depend on the same continuously re-evaluated geometry that live impedance checking first required the tool to maintain.

title: Incident Postmortem: The Hybrid-Dielectric Stackup Misprediction
text: In October 2024, a customer building a co-packaged-optics interconnect board on a hybrid low-loss dielectric stackup discovered during post-fabrication bring-up that six of forty-eight 112 Gbps PAM4 channels failed eye-diagram margin by a wide enough amount to be unusable, despite every channel having passed Striplane's v7.0 stackup-optimizer-recommended design cleanly during pre-fab sign-off. The customer's bring-up team initially suspected a fabrication defect, but time-domain reflectometry measurements on the failing channels showed measured characteristic impedance running roughly 12% lower than Striplane's predicted values, well outside the tool's own ±5% Class IV tolerance band, while passing channels on the same board matched prediction within 3%. Investigation traced the discrepancy to the v7.0 machine-learning-assisted stackup optimizer, introduced earlier that year, which had recommended a specific resin-to-glass ratio for the hybrid dielectric material that the optimizer's training data did not actually cover; the optimizer had been trained almost entirely on standard FR-4 and one common low-loss laminate, and it extrapolated a dielectric-constant prediction for the hybrid material well outside the range its training data supported, without any signal that the prediction was an extrapolation rather than an interpolation. Because the closed-form impedance solver trusted the optimizer's dielectric-constant output directly, the resulting impedance predictions looked internally consistent and passed every DRC check, even though the underlying material assumption was wrong. Remediation began with an emergency patch, shipped as v7.3 in November 2024, that added a confidence score to every stackup-optimizer recommendation, flagging any material combination falling outside a defined similarity radius of the training data as low-confidence and requiring manual physical TDR correlation before the recommendation could be marked sign-off-ready. In parallel, the team retrained the optimizer on an expanded data set incorporating measurements from eleven additional dielectric materials, including the specific hybrid laminate involved in the incident, released as part of v8.0 in January 2025. A secondary process change required any board using a stackup material combination not previously validated in Striplane's calibration database to complete at least one physical TDR correlation run before the board's design was eligible for production release, regardless of confidence score. The customer's affected board was rerouted using a corrected stackup and passed full qualification on the second fabrication run, though the incident delayed the product's launch by approximately seven weeks. Striplane's team now publishes a quarterly list of materials newly added to the optimizer's validated training set, so customers evaluating an unfamiliar dielectric can check in advance whether the optimizer's recommendation for that material carries a low-confidence flag before committing to a fabrication run.

title: Appendix: Glossary — Differential Impedance Tolerance
text: Differential impedance tolerance: the allowed deviation between a differential pair's simulated characteristic impedance and its assigned target, expressed as a percentage band around a nominal ohm value. The current release checks controlled-impedance nets against a default differential target of 100 ohms ±5%, tightened from the ±10% single-ended tolerance shipped in the original v1.0 release of March 2018. Tighter tolerance bands generally apply to higher board classes, as summarized in the board-class impedance table elsewhere in this document. A net whose simulated impedance falls outside its assigned tolerance band is reported as a DRC violation regardless of how far outside the band the measured value falls, since Striplane does not currently distinguish marginal from severe tolerance violations in its default report.

title: Cross-Referencing the Crosstalk Threshold in German-Language Documentation
text: Cross-referencing external documentation occasionally surfaces version drift between Striplane's English-language specification and translated summaries circulated by partner integrators. A German-language integration bulletin describing Striplane's crosstalk defaults speaks of "8 Prozent des Spitze-Spitze-Hubs des Störsignals"; rendered back into English, the bulletin's rule reads: "Striplane's crosstalk engine flags any coupled-noise ratio exceeding 8% of the aggressor's peak-to-peak swing for nets routed closer than 3x trace width over a parallel-run length beyond 500 mils." The bulletin restates the same rule described in the crosstalk engine section but was translated from an interim internal build note rather than the current shipping specification. Integrators relying on translated bulletins for configuration defaults should confirm current thresholds directly against the English-language release notes rather than a partner-translated summary, since translated bulletins are not guaranteed to track every point release.

title: FAQ: Eye-Diagram Bit-Error-Rate Target
text: What bit-error-rate target does Striplane's eye-diagram check use by default? Eye-diagram verification in Striplane v5.0 and later drives a virtual bit-error-rate target of 1e-12 at the receiver, sampled across a minimum eye height of 30 mV and eye width of 0.30 UI for 56 Gbps PAM4 links, per the tool's default mask library. This default can be overridden per net class in the mask-library configuration file if a customer's link budget specifies a different target. Loosening the bit-error-rate target without also adjusting eye height and width independently is not recommended, since the three parameters are calibrated together against the same reference channel model. Support tickets asking about this default most often come from teams porting an existing mask library from another verification tool.

title: EMI and the Return-Path Connection
text: Radiated emissions problems on high-speed digital boards trace back more often to return-path discontinuities than to the signal traces engineers usually suspect first. Striplane's EMI estimator does not attempt a full electromagnetic simulation of the finished board; instead it scores each net's return-path quality, counting plane-split crossings, unstitched via transitions, and connector breakouts lacking a nearby ground pin, then converts that score into a rough radiated-emissions risk ranking rather than an absolute field-strength prediction. The estimator deliberately avoids promising a certification-grade emissions number, since an actual chamber measurement depends on enclosure geometry, cable routing, and other factors entirely outside a bare board's control. What the estimator reliably catches is the return-path geometry itself: a signal via missing a nearby stitching via, or a high-speed net crossing a plane split without a bridging capacitor nearby, both patterns strongly correlated with emissions failures in Striplane's own field-return data.

title: Cloud Verification Farm, Q2 2026
text: As of the v9.4 release, Striplane's cloud verification farm processes an average of 41,000 DRC jobs per month across customer accounts, with a median queue wait of 18 seconds and a 99th-percentile job completion time of 6.4 minutes for boards under 20 layers. The largest single verification job recorded to date checked a 32-layer, 46,000-net supercomputer interconnect backplane, completing full impedance, crosstalk, and via-stub analysis in 58 minutes. Seventy-eight percent of active customer accounts now run automated nightly DRC sweeps triggered directly from their PCB CAD tool via Striplane's version-9 API, up from 22% under the pre-cloud v8.x release line. Support tickets tagged "false positive impedance flag" fell 61% between the v8.4 and v9.4 releases after the ML-assisted stackup optimizer was retrained on an expanded field-data set.
